title: Minutes of Meeting 1
text:
# 1 Minutes of Meeting of Visual Analytics Project AY 25-26 JAN
Date of Meeting      : 27 February 2026
Location                   : LKS Library 4-16 
Time                           : 19:00 – 20:30
Attendees                 :  Members
                                    Mr. DODDAPANENI Virinchi                      DV
                                    Mr. SU Bo-Han                                             BH
                                    Mr. YU Dong Hui                                          DH
Notes of Minutes






















Item

Notes

Action













1

Discussion of ideal data sets

















BH: I prefer the energy sector data from CEIC because it provides unique industry-specific trends for our study.

















DH: I prefer the professor’s fintech dataset as it represents a comprehensive population study of 48,723 real active customers over 12 months.

















DV: This fintech dataset has a stronger analytic direction because it uniquely integrates 3.1 million transactions with app engagement and satisfaction scores.



















COFINFAD Fintech dataset.

Because its multidimensional design allows us to visualize complex relationships between product usage, digital behavior, and customer churn.














2

Discuss about the analytics strategy

















BH: proposed using K-means Clustering to segment customers into high-value, inactive, and high/low spending groups.

















DV: suggested incorporating time-related analysis, with the primary objective centered on Churn and Retention.

















DH: concurred that this direction fully leverages the transaction-level depth of the dataset.















3

Shiny App Design

















BH: suggested developing 6 modules (2 per member).

















DH: emphasized that every visualization module must include a dedicated “Insights” or observation section at the bottom to explain the findings.

















DV: recommended ensuring a logical flow between modules and adding a final summary report page.















4

Tools & Workflow

















DV: recommended using Quarto for the project website and proposal development.

















DV: proposed using Tableau to perform a quick correlation scan of the 50+ variables before formal coding in R.















5

Next Meeting day



















Confirmed Date/Time: 6 March 2026 at 18:00.

df_transactions <- df_transactions %>%
  mutate(date = as.Date(date))


df_tx_agg <- df_transactions %>%
  group_by(customer_id) %>%
  summarise(
    raw_tx_count = n(),
    raw_total_amount = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE),
    raw_avg_amount = mean(amount, na.rm = TRUE),
    raw_median_amount = median(amount, na.rm = TRUE),
    raw_sd_amount = sd(amount, na.rm = TRUE),
    raw_min_amount = min(amount, na.rm = TRUE),
    raw_max_amount = max(amount, na.rm = TRUE),
    raw_first_tx_date = min(date, na.rm = TRUE),
    raw_last_tx_date = max(date, na.rm = TRUE),
    raw_payment_ratio = sum(type == "Payment", na.rm = TRUE) / n(),
    raw_transfer_ratio = sum(type == "Transfer", na.rm = TRUE) / n(),
    raw_withdrawal_ratio = sum(type == "Withdrawal", na.rm = TRUE) / n(),
    raw_active_months = n_distinct(month(date))
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

final_df <- df_customers %>%
  left_join(df_tx_agg, by = "customer_id")

final_df <- final_df %>%
  mutate(age_group = case_when(
    age < 20 ~ "Under 20",
    age >= 20 & age < 30 ~ "20-29",
    age >= 30 & age < 40 ~ "30-39",
    age >= 40 & age < 50 ~ "40-49",
    age >= 50 & age < 60 ~ "50-59",
    age >= 60 ~ "60 and above",
    TRUE ~ "Unknown" 
  )) %>%
  mutate(age_group = factor(age_group, levels = c("Under 20", "20-29", "30-39", "40-49", "50-59", "60 and above", "Unknown")))

missing_values_by_age <- function(origin, vari = "age_group"){

}

  mutate(
    complaint_topics = replace_na(complaint_topics, "No Complaints"),
    feature_requests = replace_na(feature_requests, "No Requests"),

      is.na(credit_utilization_ratio) & (credit_card == 0 | is.na(credit_card)) ~ 0,
      TRUE ~ credit_utilization_ratio
    )
  )

title: Clustering Analysis
text:
Load Packages and Data

We begin by initializing the analytical environment with the tidyverse and poLCA ecosystems, ensuring a robust framework for data manipulation and latent class modeling.


pacman::p_load(tidyverse, poLCA, scales, patchwork, lubridate, knitr, dplyr)
df_raw <- read_csv("data/colombia_customers_processed.csv")



Latent Class Analysis (LCA)

Feature selection is focused on two dimensions: demographic traits (e.g., income, occupation) and behavioral indicators (e.g., churn probability, satisfaction) to build a holistic segmentation framework.


df_sampled <- df_raw %>%
  dplyr::select(
    gender, income_bracket, occupation, age_group,        
    total_transaction_volume, churn_probability, satisfaction_score 
  )



Data Validation & Cleaning

To maintain the integrity of our statistical inferences, we perform a rigorous quality check to ensure the dataset is free of missing values before proceeding to model estimation.


# ensure no missing values
print(sapply(df_sampled, function(x) sum(is.na(x))))

                  gender           income_bracket               occupation 
                       0                        0                        0 
               age_group total_transaction_volume        churn_probability 
                       0                        0                        0 
      satisfaction_score 
                       0 




Representative Sampling

A random sample of 5,000 observations is extracted to achieve an optimal balance between computational efficiency and the statistical power required for representative results.


set.seed(123) 
df_sampled <- df_sampled %>% sample_n(5000)



Binning & Factorization

As LCA requires categorical inputs, continuous variables like transaction volume are discretized into ranked tiers, transforming raw metrics into meaningful behavioral levels.


df_sampled <- df_sampled %>%
  mutate(
    vol_level = ntile(total_transaction_volume, 3), 
    churn_level = ntile(churn_probability, 3),
    sat_level = case_when(
      satisfaction_score <= 4 ~ 1,
      satisfaction_score <= 7 ~ 2,
      TRUE ~ 3
    )
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(-total_transaction_volume, -churn_probability, -satisfaction_score)
df_lca_final <- df_sampled %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), factor)) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), as.integer))



Determining Optimal K - BIC Plot

Model selection is guided by the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC). We iterate through multiple cluster counts to identify the “elbow” where model fit and parsimony are perfectly balanced.


f <- as.formula("cbind(gender, income_bracket, occupation, age_group, vol_level, churn_level, sat_level) ~ 1")

bic_list <- c()
for(i in 2:5){
  res <- poLCA(f, df_lca_final, nclass=i, nrep=2, maxiter=2000, verbose=FALSE)
  bic_list[i] <- res$bic
}
plot(2:5, bic_list[2:5], type="b", pch=19, col="#2E86C1", 
     main="Model Selection: BIC Plot", xlab="Number of Clusters", ylab="BIC Score")










Final Model Execution

With the optimal K identified, we execute the final model to assign each customer to their most probable latent class, providing the basis for our segment profiles.


set.seed(123)
lca_best <- poLCA(f, df_lca_final, nclass=3, nrep=10, maxiter=5000, verbose=FALSE)

df_results <- df_lca_final %>%
  mutate(cluster = as.factor(lca_best$predclass))



Clustering Analysis Dashboard

This visualization translates complex model outputs into actionable insights, mapping the average behavioral signatures of each cluster to define their unique market characteristics.


p1 <- df_results %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  summarise(across(c(vol_level, churn_level, sat_level), mean)) %>%
  pivot_longer(-cluster, names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Value") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Variable, y = Value, color = cluster, group = cluster)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.5, alpha = 0.8) + 
  geom_point(size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(1, 3), breaks = 1:3, labels = c("Low", "Med", "High")) +
  labs(title = "Cluster Behavioral Profiles", 
       subtitle = "Line plots showing the average level of key metrics",
       x = "Behavioral Variable", y = "Average Level") +
  theme_minimal()

Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.

print(p1)









lookup_table <- df_sampled %>%
  mutate(occ_id = as.integer(as.factor(occupation))) %>%
  dplyr::select(occupation, occ_id) %>%
  distinct()

p2_v2 <- df_results %>%

  left_join(lookup_table, by = c("occupation" = "occ_id")) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = cluster, fill = occupation.y)) + 
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Detailed Occupation Mix", 
       subtitle = "Who are these people? (By real job titles)",
       x = "Cluster", y = "Percentage Share", fill = "Job Title") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.text = element_text(size = 8))

print(p2_v2)









get_mode <- function(v) {
  uniqv <- unique(v)
  uniqv[which.max(tabulate(match(v, uniqv)))]
}
persona_summary <- df_results %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  summarise(
    Segment_Size = n(),
    Top_Occupation = get_mode(occupation), 
    Top_Age_Group = get_mode(age_group),
    Top_Gender = get_mode(gender),
    Avg_Volume_Level = round(mean(as.integer(vol_level)), 1),
    Avg_Churn_Risk = round(mean(as.integer(churn_level)), 1),
    Avg_Satisfaction = round(mean(as.integer(sat_level)), 1)
  )

print(persona_summary)

Cluster 1: Elite Loyalists (Size: 1,344)

Profile: High-skilled professionals (Engineers, Doctors, Lawyers).
Insight: Despite being high-value, they have the lowest satisfaction (1.16). They are “silent sufferers” who stay out of necessity but are unhappy with the service.




Cluster 2: At-Risk Dissatisfieds (Size: 1,382)

Profile: Service sector workers (Sales, Waiters, Mechanics).
Insight: The most critical group with the highest churn risk (2.86/3.0) and low satisfaction. They are actively looking to leave the bank.

Cluster 3: Stable Casual Users (Size: 2,274)

Profile: Stable middle-class roles (Teachers, Accountants, Architects).
Insight: The most reliable segment. They exhibit the lowest churn risk (1.45) and the highest relative satisfaction, serving as the bank’s stable revenue base.

anova.model <- function(min_split, complexity_parameter, max_depth) {

Random Forest

Splitting of data set into train vs. test data

set.seed(1234)
trainIndex <- createDataPartition(df$churn_probability, 
                                  p = 0.8,      
                                  list = FALSE, 
                                  times = 1)
df_train <- df[trainIndex, ]  
df_test  <- df[-trainIndex, ]



Hyperparameter Tuning and Training of Model

repeatcvControl <- trainControl(
  method = "repeatedcv",
  number = 10,
  repeats = 3,          
  verboseIter = TRUE     
)

n_features <- ncol(df_train) - 1      
my_mtry <- floor(sqrt(n_features))  

rf_model <- train(
  churn_probability ~ .,            
  data = df_train,
  method = "ranger", 
  trControl = repeatcvControl, 
  num.trees = 50,                  
  importance = "impurity", 
  tuneGrid = data.frame(
    mtry = my_mtry, 
    min.node.size = 5,
    splitrule = "variance"
  )
)

Aggregating results
Fitting final model on full training set



df_test$fit_forest <- predict(rf_model, df_test)
test_r2 <- cor(df_test$churn_probability, df_test$fit_forest)^2
rf_scatter <- ggplot(data = df_test, aes(x = churn_probability, y = fit_forest)) + 
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "darkgreen") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "Actual Churn Probability", 
       y = "Predicted Churn Probability",
       title = paste0("Test R-squared: ", round(test_r2, digits = 3))) + 
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 8),
        axis.title = element_text(size = 10),
        title = element_text(size = 10))
rf_residuals <- ggplot(data = df_test, aes(x = churn_probability, 
                                          y = (fit_forest - churn_probability))) + 
  geom_point(col = "blue3", alpha = 0.4) +
  labs(y = "Residuals (Error)", x = "Actual Churn Probability") + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, col = "red4", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) + 
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 8),
        axis.title = element_text(size = 10))

library(patchwork)
p <- rf_scatter + rf_residuals + 
  plot_annotation(title = "Model Performance Analysis: Churn Probability Prediction",
                  subtitle = "Left: Predicted vs Actual | Right: Residual Distribution",
                  theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold")))

for (i in tree_range){
  rf_tmp <- train(
    churn_probability ~ ., 
    data = df_train,
    method = "ranger", 
    trControl = trctrl_none, 
    num.trees = i,
    importance = "impurity", 
    tuneGrid = data.frame(mtry = my_mtry, min.node.size = 5, splitrule = "variance")

  rsquared_trees <- append(rsquared_trees, rf_tmp$finalModel$r.squared)
}

  data_cleaned,
  aes(
    x = factor(active_products),
    y = churn_probability,
    fill = factor(active_products)
  )
) +
  geom_boxplot(show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Churn Probability by Number of Active Products",
    x = "Number of Active Products",
    y = "Churn Probability"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
